title: A System Aware Resource Allocation for Distributed Workflows in Quantum Computing Environments
authors: Abhishek Sawaika, Udaya Parampalli, Rajkumar Buyya
abstract: Rapid advancements in cloud based platforms providing access to quantum computing capabilities have opened up several challenges for efficient usage of these highly delicate and costly devices. Although most of the current systems use a priority based access protocol, they are unable to fully support reliable, efficient, and scalable execution of larger-scale applications. To overcome this limitation, we propose a comprehensive solution for efficient allocation of quantum programs to appropriate quantum devices, considering all the relevant cost metrics into account including, fidelity, execution time and communication overhead. We also formulate use-cases for distributed quantum workflow and propose modified graph based algorithms to solve for allocation of such use-cases, assuming a hybrid classical-quantum network. Since hardware advancements in large standalone devices is an ongoing process, it is critical to investigate such distributed workflows to maximize the best utilization of current NISQ devices. Our empirical study shows that the proposed techniques perform better than state-of-the-art methods for almost all evaluation parameters, with average improvements of approximately $5\%$ in execution time, $30\%$ in communication overhead, $40\%$ in wait time and $2\%$ in fidelity, providing better solutions to efficient allocation strategies.
summary: This paper proposes new algorithms for managing distributed quantum computing tasks in the cloud. By optimizing for fidelity, time, and network costs, the methods significantly reduce delays and communication overhead in NISQ-era distributed networks.
main_problem: Efficient resource allocation for distributed quantum workflows in cloud-based environments, specifically addressing the limitations of priority-based protocols in handling large-scale, multi-task applications.
main_result: The proposed SoftIso and RandomAware algorithms achieve significant improvements in execution time (~5%), communication overhead (~30%), and wait time (~40%) compared to existing methods like CloudQC.
method: The study introduces SoftIso, an extended graph isomorphism-based matching algorithm with early stopping, and RandomAware, a randomized selection algorithm, to optimize a multi-objective cost function.
model_or_system: A hybrid classical-quantum network architecture consisting of a Quantum Resource Manager and distributed quantum nodes (QPUs) with specific error models, connectivity, and quantum memory.
key_observables: Fidelity, execution time, communication overhead, wait time, and task completion percentage.
important_parameters: Qubit count, circuit depth, gate counts (1-qubit and 2-qubit), readout error, CLOPS, and network latency.
main_assumptions: The study assumes a hybrid quantum-classical network rather than a purely quantum network and focuses on allocating standalone programs rather than partitioning single large circuits.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows a GHZ state circuit; Figure 2 depicts the cloud service architecture; Table I compares the proposed method to others; Figures 5-9 show trends in communication cost, fidelity, workload distribution, and execution/wait times.
paper_structure: The paper defines the scientific problem, establishes the system architecture and mathematical model, introduces the proposed SoftIso and RandomAware algorithms, details the simulation framework using IBM hardware data, and concludes with performance analysis and scalability results.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1: A sample quantum circuit (GHZ state preparation) with 5 qubits, depth=6, 1 single qubit operation (H), four 2- qubits operations (CNOT) and 5 measurements, representing a quantum program.
Fig. 3: Sample quantum workflows of different sizes, where ’N’ denote the number to tasks in each workflow.
Fig. 4: Process pipeline for experimental simulation
Fig. 5: Trends for communication cost by (a) workload size for allocation, (b) number of nodes in the network and (c) distinct tasks per workflow
